title: NIS2 Supply Chain Third-Party Risk Continuous Monitoring
exclusions: Does not evaluate the depth of supplier security assessments, interpret Implementing Regulation proportionality criteria, approve vendor relationships, or replace procurement or counsel review. Only monitors traffic volumes and assessment overdue dates.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.16` daily exports, ES asset snapshot IDs, and frozen bucket copies referenced in the third-party risk workpaper.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(d) requires supply chain security. The UC continuously monitors third-party network traffic volumes and assessment overdue dates for categorised suppliers. It cannot evaluate the quality of supplier security controls or contractual clauses, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.16: NIS2 Supply Chain Third-Party Risk Continuous Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.16' running on index proxy and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Load accelerated fixtures where `All_Traffic.dest_category=supplier`, `bytes_out` exceeds one gigabyte per day, and `supplier_risk_lookup` shows `last_assessment_date` older than 400 days; the alert fires listing dest and overdue flag 1.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Feed the same traffic volumes but provide an updated assessment date within 90 days and a documented marketing campaign exception row; the search returns no rows after applying the lookup filter and exception table.

dataSources: Accelerated CIM `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` fed from `index=proxy` `sourcetype=bluecoat:proxystorage` or `sourcetype=zscaler:lss` with fields dest, bytes_out, src, app; `index=cloud` `sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail` (Splunk Add-on for AWS [Splunkbase 1876](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1876)) for partner-shared buckets; Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)) provides `dest_category=supplier` tagging via Assets & Identities.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)), Splunk Add-on for AWS ([Splunkbase 1876](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1876)); optional Splunk Add-on for Cisco Umbrella for DNS enrichment.
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out dc(All_Traffic.src) as internal_sources
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where All_Traffic.dest_category="supplier"
  by All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.app span=1d
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| lookup supplier_risk_lookup dest OUTPUT supplier_name, risk_tier, last_assessment_date
| eval days_since_assessment=round((now()-strptime(last_assessment_date,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400, 0)
| eval assessment_overdue=if(days_since_assessment > 365, 1, 0)
| where bytes_out > 1073741824 OR assessment_overdue=1 OR internal_sources > 50
| sort - bytes_out
| table dest, supplier_name, risk_tier, bytes_out, internal_sources, days_since_assessment, assessment_overdue
description: Uses accelerated network-traffic summaries tagged for supplier destinations to highlight oversized daily uploads, overdue third-party risk reviews, or an unusually wide set of internal clients talking to one supplier API.
value: Risk committees see spikes in data movement toward a vendor before a news-driven audit question lands, and they get a calendar view of which supplier reassessments slipped past 12 months without paperwork.
implementation: (1) In ES (263), assign `category=supplier` to third-party CIDRs/domains under **Configure → Data Enrichment → Asset Management**. (2) Build `supplier_risk_lookup` CSV with dest (IP or FQDN), supplier_name, risk_tier, last_assessment_date. (3) Enable **Network_Traffic** datamodel acceleration for **30 days** minimum. (4) Validate proxy field mapping to `All_Traffic.bytes_out`. (5) Add AWS CloudTrail (1876) panels if suppliers use cross-account roles.
visualization: Table of supplier rows with bytes_out and assessment flags, bar chart of top destinations, single value count of overdue assessments.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
knownFalsePositives:
• **Large overnight backup to a supplier-managed SaaS region** — Distinguish: backup software User-Agent known from allow list. Suppress: lookup `backup_supplier_dest.csv` with dest,app tuple.
• **Assessment date CSV stale while GRC tool holds truth** — Distinguish: `last_assessment_date` older than 400 days but Workday shows recent review. Suppress: sync automation from ServiceNow GRC; interim time-bound exception register.
• **CDN front door shared by many suppliers** — Distinguish: single `dest` maps to generic edge IP. Suppress: refine Assets to use **Fully Qualified Domain Name** from TLS SNI via Stream Add-on.
• **AWS cross-account telemetry mirrored into supplier category** — Distinguish: role assumption for telemetry, not data exfiltration. Suppress: `where All_Traffic.app!="sensor-forwarder"` after validation.
• **Seasonal e-invoicing traffic spike** — Distinguish: finance window predictable from calendar. Suppress: `where NOT (date_month=3 AND app="sap")` macro during close.
equipmentModels: Bluecoat/Symantec ProxySG (sourcetype bluecoat:proxystorage, bytes_out for upload monitoring)
Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876, CloudTrail cross-account role assumption events)
Splunk CIM Add-on (Splunkbase 1621, Network_Traffic data model normalisation)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, Assets & Identities for dest_category tagging)
Zscaler Internet Access (sourcetype zscaler:lss, ZIA log streaming for cloud proxy telemetry)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `supplier_risk_lookup` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('supplier_risk_lookup')`.
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(d)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.16: NIS2 Supply Chain Third-Party Risk Continuous Monitoring." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


7 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** In ES. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** , assign `category=supplier` to third-party CIDRs/domains under **Configure → Data Enrichment → Asset Management**. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Build `supplier_risk_lookup` CSV with dest (IP or FQDN), supplier_name, risk_tier, last_assessment_date. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Enable **Network_Traffic** datamodel acceleration for **30 days** minimum. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** Validate proxy field mapping to `All_Traffic.bytes_out`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 6:** Add AWS CloudTrail. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 7:** panels if suppliers use cross-account roles. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Supply Chain Third-Party Risk Continuous Monitoring," the aggregation produces the risk posture — the per-row values should reconcile to the organisation's risk register entries, not stand alone as engineering metrics.

This search uses `| tstats` against a CIM-accelerated data model rather than reading raw events. The performance delta is typically 10-50x over raw search, and for large indexes can be >100x. The acceleration summaries are maintained on a rolling window (default: 1 year, configurable in the data model editor) and are queried from indexer-local summary files rather than re-scanning raw events.

CIM acceleration dependency: this search FAILS SILENTLY if the underlying data model is not accelerated. It will return zero rows instead of raising an error, which is exactly the wrong failure mode for a compliance detection. Verify acceleration status before scheduling:

```spl
| rest /services/admin/summarization
| search title="<datamodel_name>"
| table title access_count summary.complete summary.earliest_time summary.latest_time
```

`summary.complete` should show 100 (or very close). If it's below 95, the acceleration is still catching up or has stalled — check the DMA audit log (`index=_audit action=dma_run`) for errors.

Aggregation choice: `sum()` is grouped by `All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.app`. This produces a summary row per distinct combination, which is typically what compliance reporting requires (one row per asset, one row per user, one row per rule). If downstream processing expects a flat event stream, avoid `tstats` — summary rows are not raw events and cannot be drilled into without re-running against the raw index.

When NOT to use tstats: if the field being filtered was added after the acceleration started, or is search-time extracted by an app the acceleration isn't aware of, the field will not exist in the summaries and the `where` clause will drop every row. Rebuild the acceleration after any field-extraction changes.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Network_Traffic` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Network_Traffic` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.16: NIS2 Supply Chain Third-Party Risk Continuous Monitoring]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.16,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `assessment_overdue`, `bytes_out`, `days_since_assessment`, `dest`, `internal_sources`, `risk_tier`, `supplier_name`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table assessment_overdue, bytes_out, days_since_assessment, dest, internal_sources, risk_tier, supplier_name
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `supplier_risk_lookup`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup supplier_risk_lookup
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_since_assessment`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_since_assessment) as p50 perc90(days_since_assessment) as p90 perc99(days_since_assessment) as p99 max(days_since_assessment) as hi
```

The current threshold `365` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.16: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Supplier Rows With Bytes Out And Assessment Flags (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Top Destinations (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value Count Of Overdue Assessments (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Zero results when acceleration is fresh:** The field you're filtering on was likely added after the acceleration started — rebuild the acceleration.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on how much information flows out to outside partners and whether anyone forgot to renew the formal safety review for those partners on time.
